BSc-Computer-Science/Concepts/IP-Address.title: IP Address
BSc-Computer-Science/Concepts/IP-Address.content:
The ISP provides the IP Addresses тЖТ 4 byte (4 * 8 bits = 32 bits) address

IPv4 and IPv6 standards


Static IP тЖТ IP Address does not change (e.g. for web services)
Dynamic IP тЖТ IP addresses change frequently (e.g. phone, laptop and personal devices)
Private IP тЖТ Valid only within a local network (unique within the network)
Public IP тЖТ Accessible anywhere on the internet (assigned to router)



Format: XX.XX.XX.XX
Hierarchy тЖТ First byte corresponds to the larger network, then the following is more and more specific
IPv6 тЖТ Expanding to 6bytes instead of only 4 bytes (but lacking infrastructure)

Lecture notes ┬з
Initial terms ┬з

Transistors тЖТ Can amplify or switch eletronic signals and eletrical power. Made from semiconductors. They can control flow of electricity by applying a small input signal

Collector тЖТ collects the current
Base тЖТ receives the input signal which turns on/off the switch
Emitter тЖТ where the current is released if the base receives an input


Current тЖТ flow of eletric charge through a current, measured in Ampere. In CS we consider the states when the current flows (1) or when it doesnтАЩt (0)
Circuit тЖТ Multiple transistors mixed together
Gate тЖТ combined circuits/transistors

AND ( . ) Gate тЖТ Both input need to be true
OR Gate (+) тЖТ Either input must be true
XOR Gate ( тКХ )тЖТ Accepts only one true input тЖТ A.BтА▓+AтА▓.B
NOT ( тАШ) тЖТ inverts the input



Abstraction in hardware design ┬з
Binary number system ┬з

Half adder тЖТ doesnтАЩt account for the carry bit





Arithmetic logic unit ┬з
Abstraction of basic circuits тЖТ Somewhat smarter than normal circuits
ALU does not have excess memory тЖТ it cannot store the processed information

Opcode (Operation code) тЖТ type of operation we want to perform
Status тЖТ whether the operation was successful
Result тЖТ Result of the operation
A & B тЖТ the two inputs of the operation



                  
                  Exam info 
                  
                
Most complicated in the exam will be basic combinational circuits such as XOR (slide 19)

Central processing unit ┬з


Arithmetic logic unit
Random Access Memory тЖТ stores data and instructions that the CPU accesses during execution. Volatile memory.
Registers тЖТ Temporary memory that CPUтАЩs have direct access to
Control unit тЖТ Receives instruction from memory and controls flow of data within the CPU. Interprets the Opcode to determine the operation to be performed by ALU or memory

Instructions set and machine code ┬з


==Instruction set== тЖТ Collections of instructions that the CPU can execute. Operations that you can perform on the computer. They are identified by a certain Opcode.

RISC тЖТ Reduced Instructions Set тЖТ mostly in phones, low computational power
CISC тЖТ Complex Instructions Set



MooreтАЩs Law ┬з


                  
                  MooreтАЩs Law 
                  
                
The number of transistors in a dense integrated circuit doubles about every two years.


We are reaching a physical limit in MooreтАЩs law

Lecture notes ┬з
Version control and Git ┬з
Functions of version control

Saves versions
Assigns unique names to each version
Maintains the relations between the various versions
Keeps track of the current version pointer

Most common actions in version control

Commit / Checkin тЖТ Current version gets updated with the changes
Dropping a version / Reverting back тЖТ move the current version back to a previous commit
Master/Main branch тЖТ тАЬOfficialтАЭ version
Repository тЖТ Structured library of a version control mechanism (local or online)
Checking out тЖТ Retrieving a read-only copy of the code
Merge тЖТ
Conflict тЖТ Issues in merging commits. Often have to be handled manually
Staging environment тЖТ included changes to be committed

How version control works

Individual file versions are independent, but the current version pointer keeps the current version of each file in sync.

Introduction ┬з

TOC Attempts to answer the question of what problems we can solve with a computer (e.g. social problems)



                  
                  Example: Halting Problem 
                  
                
Given a piece of code, can you write a piece code that can determine wether the first program will run indefinitely or not?
This problem was first proposed by Alan Turing

Automata ┬з


                  
                  Automaton 
                  
                
An Automaton is an idealized mathematical computing machine.
Usually used to solve relatively simple logical problems.


Why build models?

==Mathematical simplicity== тЖТ easier to manipulate models than actual computers
Intellectual robustness тЖТ All computers can be thought of as special cases of a general model

Definition of problems ┬з
We need a definition of problems with specific characteristics:

Corresponds to the problem we want to solve
Captures a large class of problems
Is mathematically simple to reason about
but no one definition encapsulates all three problems

Finite automata ┬з


                  
                  Finite Automata 
                  
                
A Finite automata are abstraction of computers with finite resources constraints

Finite State Automata:

Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)
Non-deterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)

Formal language theory ┬з
Important terms ┬з

Alphabet тЖТ Set of characters
Characters тЖТ Individual symbols, element of an alphabet
Strings тЖТ Finite sequence of characters
Language тЖТ Set of strings


String ┬з

Informal definitionтЖТ Any sequence of any symbols (characters)
Formal definition тЖТ

An alphabet (╬г) is a finite set of symbols called characters
A string over an alphabet ╬г is a finite sequence of characters drawn from ╬г

For example, If ╬г={a,b}, a valid string over ╬г is ababababaaababab or a


The empty string has no characters and is denoted by ╧╡



Language ┬з

Informal definitionтЖТ A set of strings
Formal definition:

A formal language is a set of strings
We say that L is a language over ╬г if it is a set of strings over ╬г
The set of all strings composed from letters in ╬г is denoted as ╬гтИЧ
Formally we say that L is a language over ╬г if LтКЖ╬гтИЧ



The model ┬з


                  
                  Fundamental question 
                  
                
For what language L can you design an automaton that takes as input a string, then determines whether the string is in L?

Finite automata ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A finite automaton is a simple type of mathematical machine for determining whether a string is contained within some language


Finite тЖТ The automata has a stopping point, this is intended to mimic computerтАЩs finite resources
==State== тЖТ
Transition тЖТ
Final/Accept State тЖТ If the automata ends in the accept state, the string is recognized and such string belongs to the automaton.

The language of an automaton is the set of strings that it accepts:

If D is an automaton that processes characters from alphabet ╬г, then L(D) is formally defined as:

L(D)={wтИИ╬гтИЧтИгD  accepts w}



FSA Examples ┬з

Deterministic finite automaton ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A DFA is defined relative to some alphabet ╬г. For each state in the DFA, there must be exactly one transaction defined for each symbol in ╬г (Determinism).
A DFA has a unique start state and there are zero or more accepting states.


Need for clearly defined routes and behavior in all cases in an Automaton
These conditions need to be disallowed

No transition out of a state on some input
Multiple transitions out of a state on some input

Deterministic finite automaton ┬з


                  
                  Formal definition 
                  
                
D=(Q,╬г,╬┤,q0тАЛ,F)

D тЖТ Automaton
Q тЖТ Set of states
╬г тЖТ alphabet
╬┤ тЖТ Transition function
q0тАЛ тЖТ Start state
F тЖТ Accepting state


Tabular DFA ┬з
Tabular representation of the transactions in a deterministic finite automaton


                  
                  Possible exam question 
                  
                
A possible exam question is to draw up the tabular representation of a DFA


Regular languages ┬з
Introduction ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A language L is called a regular language if there exists a DFA D such that L(D)=L
If L is a language and L(D) = L, we say that D recognizes the language L


Complement of a language ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
Given a language LтКЖ╬гтИЧ, the complement of that language (LтАЩ) is the language of all strings in ╬гтИЧ that arenтАЩt in L:
LтА▓=╬гтИЧтИТL

Complementing regular languages ┬з

L={wтИИ{a,b}тИЧтИг w contains aa as a substring }
L={wтИИ{a,b}тИЧтИг w does not contain aa as a substring }

Closure properties ┬з


                  
                  Theorem 
                  
                
If L is a regular language, then LтАЩ is also a regular language.
As a result, we say that the regular languages are *closed under complementation

Nondeterministic finite automaton ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
An NFA is a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton. Structurally similar to a DFA, but represents a fundamental shift in how weтАЩll think about computation


Nondeterministic computational model тЖТ Computing machine may have multiple decisions that it can make at one point. The machine accepts if any series of choices leads to an accepting state (Existential nondetermi anism)

Example of an NFA:

тЖТ The only difference in the formal definition of a NFA from an DFA is the transition function.
NFA Acceptance ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
An NFA N accepts a string w if there is some series of choices that lead to an accepting state.
An NFA rejects a string w if no possible series of choices lead it into an accepting state.


There is a difference between reaching a non-accepting state and to die:

NFA Rejecting a string тЖТ all inputs are processed but the NFA ends in a non-accepting state
NFA Dies тЖТ When executing a string, the NFA reaches a point where it can not process further input



╧╡- Transitions ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
An NFA may follow any number of ╧╡-transitions at any time without consuming any input. NFAs are not required to follow such transitions.

Designing NFAs ┬з

Guess and check method is enough

Difference between NFAs and DFAs ┬з

Any language that can be accepted by a DFA can be accepted by an NFA

(Every DFA is technically an NFA)


Any language accepted by an NFA can also be accepted by a DFA

Introduction to Networks ┬з


Communication networks тЖТ to exchange data and information

Data тЖТ database of values
Information тЖТ what is extracted from processed data



ShannonтАЩs Communication Model тЖТ Devised by Claude Shannon in 1948





Classification of networks by extension

PAN (Personal area network) тЖТ Serves a single individual wirelessly
LAN (Local area network) тЖТ connects machines in a single building
WAN (Wide area network) тЖТ large areas, are made up by smaller networks



Communication channels тЖТ medium for information transmission

Wired channels тЖТ wires or cables

Pros тЖТ Shileded, dependable and secure
Cons тЖТ costly, limited mobility and easy to damage


Wireless channels

Used means тЖТ Radio signals, microwave
Radio Frequency тЖТ Sent by transceivers through antennas
Wireless devices and transceivers тЖТ send and receive data and include antennas





Security in wired channels тЖТ Wired connections are secure


Bandwidth in communication channels

Bandwidth тЖТ transmission capacity
Broadband тЖТ at least 25 Mbps
Narrowband тЖТ Slower than 25 Mbps



Topology of Networks тЖТ structure and layout of network components


Point-to-point тЖТ connects peripheral devices to a host
Star topology тЖТ Connects devices to a central device
Mesh topology тЖТ All the devices are connected with each other



Network nodes

Network node тЖТ any device on a network
Data Terminal Equipment тЖТ stores or generates data
Data Communication Equipment тЖТ controls data speed, signal conversion, error checking and routing



Devices as Network Nodes

Router тЖТ controls data flow, gateway, communication between multiple networks
Modem тЖТ converts signals for communication channels
Switch тЖТ communication within a single network



Introduction to the internet ┬з

Internet тЖТ global system of computers connected together

Redundancy тЖТ The internet is redundant (Redundancy of paths)
Hierarchical тЖТ internet is organized into levels/groups


Birth of the internet тЖТ 1957 project at Stanford to connect computing machines at a distance as a part of the ARPA project. This later expanded to connect all universities in the US and include multiple services.
Client-Server Architecture

Checkout https://traceroute-online.com
tracerout [website]

How does the receiving computer interpret the response

how to ensure all information was transferred
how to handle multiple requests and responses to the same router
How do routers know where to send information



TCP/IP Protocol ┬з

Defines rules for sending and receiving information between devices
First developed by the US Department of Defense, now used widely
Layers of the protocol:

Network Layer тЖТ Captures the physical aspects of data transmission and hardware related protocols.
Internet Layer тЖТ Looks after the logical transmission of data. Defines an IP Address for each device connected to the network.
Transport Layer тЖТ Responsible for end-to-end communication and handles error-free transfers (TCP and UDP protocols). Some data has higher importance than other and is thus handled differently (e.g. bank info)
Application Layer тЖТ Where the server defines networking preferences and safety (SSL)



Application Layer Protocols ┬з

Hypertext Transfer Protocol тЖТ for web browsing, defines how web browsers and web servers communicate

200s тЖТ all good
400s тЖТ client errors
500s тЖТ server errors


Languages: HTML, CSS, JavaScript

Transport Layer Protocols ┬з

Responsible for end-to-end communication and data integrtiy
Handles data segmentation, sequencing, error correction and flow control
Transmission Control Protocol тЖТ ensures reliable communication, confirms data receipt and retransmits lost data
User Datagram Protocol тЖТ Faster but less reliable, suitable for real-time application and video-streaming

Internet Protocols & IP Addresses ┬з

Devices connected to the internet need to adhere to the IP protocol

Network Layer ┬з

Handles the physical aspect of communication
Specifies and defines cable types, signal standards and data rates
Ethernet тЖТ wired connection with twisted-pair or coaxial cables
Wi-Fi тЖТ standard for wireless connections, using radio frequency tech

Tier 1 ISP networks form the backbone of the internet (around 16 globally)

Redistribution тЖТ redirects requests and responses through routers to avoid traffic
Caching тЖТ saves frequently requested resources
Load balancing тЖТ stores resources across multiple servers to manage load



Distributed Systems ┬з

Aim at solving the high expenses of memory and CPU usage of certain processes
Linking тАЬcheaperтАЭ computers into a bigger machine тЖТ each smaller machine solves or stores part of the problem

BSc-Computer-Science/Period-1/BCS1130-Di.title: BCS1130 Discrete mathematics
BSc-Computer-Science/Period-1/BCS1130-Di.content:
Course description ┬з
In this course, we build a mathematical framework that is based on logic and reason. The main objective of the course is to make students familiar with the language of mathematics. Students will learn how to make sound arguments and to detect where and why certain arguments go wrong. For this purpose, we will discuss the basic principles of logic and, closely related, the basic types of mathematical proofs. In doing so, we will encounter numbers such as integers, natural numbers and real numbers and we shall examine what makes these numbers special. After that, we will use basic logic to discuss, among other things, the following mathematical concepts: infinity, sets, relations, functions, permutations and combinations. Our fundamental tool in all of this is plain common sense. You really do not need your toolbox of mathematical formulas learned in previous studies and neither do you need a calculator. Pen and paper are the basic instruments needed. After completing each topic, exercises will be provided to be completed in class or at home, since mathematics is mainly learned by practising repeatedly.

Mathematical induction theory ┬з


                  
                  Mathematical induction 
                  
                
Proof technique for statements of the form тИАnтЙеN,nтИИZ (set of integers from a starting point on, e.g. natural numbers)


Two steps in the proof by induction:

Base case (proof of P(n)) тЖТ the statement holds for the first number
Induction step: proof of тИАnтЙеN(P(n)тЖТP(n+1))


Process of induction

P(n) is true тЖТ base case
P(n)тЖТP(n+1)тИАn тЖТ (induction step)
P(n),P(n)тЖТP(n+1)тЯ╣P(n+1)



Induction example ┬з


                  
                  Induction example 
                  
                
тИАnтИИN(тИСk+1nтАЛk=2n(n+1)тАЛ)
Base case: Proof of P(1)

тИСk=11тАЛ=1=21(1+1)тАЛ=1 тЖТ This holds

** Induction step**: Proof of тИАmтЙе1,p(m)тЖТP(m+1)

p(m):тИСk=1mтАЛk=2m(m+1)тАЛ (assumption)
p(m+1):тИСk=1m+1тАЛk=2(m+1)(m+2)тАЛ (to prove)



тИСk=1m+1тАЛk=(1+2+тАж.+m+(m+1))

we see that (1+2+тАж+m)=p(m):тИСk=1mтАЛk=2m(m+1)тАЛ


We use our assumption

тИСk=1m+1тАЛk=(1+2+тАж.+m+(m+1))=2m(m+1)тАЛ+(m+1)
=(m+1)(2mтАЛ+1)=(m+1)(2m+2тАЛ)



Induction example 2 ┬з


                  
                  Induction example 2 
                  
                
тИАnтИИN:4nтИТ1 is divisible by 3
Base case: Proof of P(1)

P(1)=41тИТ1=3 тЖТ 3 is divisible by 3$

Induction step: Proof of P(x)тЖТP(x+1)тИАxтИИN

P(x):4xтИТ1 is divisible by 3 (assumption / hypothesis)
P(x+1):4x+1тИТ1 is divisible by 3 (to prove)


Always use your assumption hypothesis!

P(x+1)=4x+1тИТ1=4тЛЕ4xтИТ1=3тЛЕ4x+4xтИТ1

4xтИТ1 is divisible by 3 by assumption (3k) тЖТ 4xтИТ1=3k
3тЛЕ4x is also a multiple of 3


P(x+1)=3тЛЕ4x+4xтИТ1=3тЛЕ4x+3k=3(4x+k) тЖТ multiple of 3

Induction example 3 ┬з


                  
                  Induction example 3 
                  
                
A country has two types of coins: 3 cents and 7 cents. Prove that you can make all sums of money starting from 12 cents (up to 1 cent accuracy) from these coins.

Explaining the example
2тЛЕ7=5тЛЕ3
Negation: max 1x7, max 1x3 тЖТ cannot add up to 12 or more

If we have 2x3 (at least), we can go 1 cent up
If we have 2x7 (at least), we can go 1 cent up
We always have to have either (or both) 2x7, or 2x3

Proof by induction

Base case: P(12) holds тЖТ 12=3+3+3+3
Inductive step: P(q)тЖТP(q+1)

q has at least 2 coins of 7 or 2 coins of 3

r: at least 2 coins of 7
s: at least 2 coins of 3
┬м(rтИиs)=┬мrтИз┬мs is impossible, as q тЙе 1


if r is true, then we can replace 3x7 by 5x3
if s is true, then we can replace 2x3 by 7


тЖТ in both cases, we can make q + 1

Relation definition ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation R describes the relationship between different elements of a given set A:
Formal: A relation R on a set A is a subset of the product set A├ЧA
xRy

Example:
A={1,2,3} тЖТ  xRy if xтЙдy
Relation diagrams ┬з


Reflective relations ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation R on a set A is reflexive if every element of A is related to itself:
тИАxтИИA:xRx


Symmetric relations ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation R on a set A is symmetric if for all elements a,b in A: aRbтЖТbRa
тИАa,bтИИA:aRbтЖТbRa


Transitive relations ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation R on a set A is transitive if for all elements a,b,b in A:
(aRbтИзbRc)тЖТaRc



Equivalence relations ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation R is called an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive


An equivalence relation corresponds to a ==partition== of the set
If you have a partition of a set, you get an equivalence relation

Example
Let A=N

xRy if xтИТy is even
Reflexive: тИАxтИИA:xRx

Let xтИИN.xтИТx=0 is even тЖТ xRx is verified тЬЕ


Symmetric: тИАx,yтИИA:xRyтЖТyRx

Assume x,yтИИN,xRy then xтИТy is even
then yтИТx is even (opposite of an even number is also even)
so yRx and the relation is symmetric тЬЕ


Transitive: тИАx,y,zтИИA:(xRyтИзyRz)тЖТxRz

Assume x,y,zтИИN,xRyтИзyRz
then xтИТy is even and yтИТz is even
then (xтИТy)+(yтИТz) is even (sum of even numbers is even)
(xтИТy)+(yтИТz)=xтИТz then xтИТz is even тЖТ xRz тЖТ Relation is transitive тЬЕ


R is reflexive, transitive and symmetric тЖТ it is an equivalence relation тЬЕ


Example ┬з


                  
                  Example Relation on R2
                  
                
R2,(x,y)R(a,b) if x+y=a+b

Is the relation reflexive? тИА(x,y)тИИR├ЧR:(x,y)R(x,y)

(x,y)R(x,y) if x+y=x+y тЖТ this is trivially true тЖТ The relation is reflexive тЬЕ

Is the relation symmetric? тИА(x1тАЛ,y2тАЛ),(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)тИИR├ЧR,(x1тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)тЖТ(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ)

Let (x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ)R(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ) then x1тАЛ+y1тАЛ=x2тАЛ+y2тАЛ
then x2тАЛ+y2тАЛ=x1тАЛ+y1тАЛ
so (x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ) тЖТ the relation is reflexive тЬЕ

Is the relation transitive? тИА(x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ),(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ),(x3тАЛ,y3тАЛ)тИИR├ЧR:(x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ)R(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)тИз(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x3тАЛ,y3тАЛ)тЖТ(x1тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x3тАЛ,y3тАЛ)

Let (x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ)R(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)тИз(x2тАЛ,y2тАЛ)R(x3тАЛ,y3тАЛ)
then x1тАЛ+y1тАЛ=x2тАЛ+y2тАЛтИзx2тАЛ+y2тАЛ=x3тАЛ+y3тАЛ
so x1тАЛ+y1тАЛ=x3тАЛ+y3тАЛ
so (x1тАЛ,y1тАЛ)R(x3тАЛ,y3тАЛ) тЖТ the relation is transitive тЬЕ


Anti-symmetry ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
It is not the negation of symmetry. A relation that cannot go in both directions

тИАx,yтИИA:(xRyтИзyRx)тЖТx=y
тИАx,yтИИA:(xRyтИзxюАа=y)тЖТx!Rx


Examples ┬з

xRy if xтЙдy on R тЖТ Anti-symmetric тЬЕ
xy if xтЙдy+10, on N

Not symmetric: x=1,y=20 then xRy, since 1тЙд20+10, but x!Ry, since 20тЙд1+10 тЭМ
Not anti-symmetric: x=1,y=2, then xRy since xтЙд2+10 and yRx since 2тЙд1+10 тЭМ

assume xRy and xюАа=y

then x=y+10тЯ╣y=xтИТ10юАа=x+10
so y!Rx





Symmetry vs. Anti-symmetry ┬з

Symmetry тЖТ all arrows go in 2 directions
==Anti-symmetry== тЖТ arrows between different elements cannot go in both directions



Partial order ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A relation that is reflexive, transitive and anti-symmetric is called a partial order

Examples ┬з

Transitive тЖТ xтЙдyтИзyтЙдzтЖТxтЙдz тЬЕ

xRy so x=ky   (kтИИN)

Not exam material


What is the difference between a partial order and a total order?

Partial order тЖТ Transitive, Anti-symmetric, reflexive

Definition of a function ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A function f is a mapping from a set A to a set B. To be a function, f(x) has a unique value for every element x of A:
f:AтЖТB,f(x)=тАж

A тЖТ Domain (asse x)

Composition of functions ┬з

Consider functions g:AтЖТB and f:BтЖТC
Then (fтИШg)(x)=f(g(x)) is the function obtained first using g and then using f

Domain of (fтИШg) тЖТ domain of g
Codomain of (fтИШg) тЖТ codomain of f



This condition must be true for the compound function: range(g)тКЖdomain(f)

Injective functions ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A function f:AтЖТB is injective if two different elements have different function values (Funzione iniettiva, every element of the domain is mapped to a different value of the codomain)
Formal definition:
A function f:AтЖТB is injective if (тИАx,yтИИA)(xюАа=yтЯ╣f(x)юАа=f(y))
Contrapositive: тИАx,yтИИA,(f(x)=f(y)тЯ╣x=y)


Proving that a function is injective: ┬з
f:NтЖТZ,f(x)=xтИТ1 тЖТ prove that it is injective

Let x,yтИИN,xюАа=y

тЯ╣xтИТ1юАа=yтИТ1
тЯ╣f(x)юАа=f(y) тЖТ Injective тЬЕ

Not injective, since 1юАа=тИТ1,f(1)=f(тИТ1) тЭМ

f:NтЖТN,f(x)=x2 тЖТ this is injective тЬЕ
Surjective functions ┬з


                  
                  Definition 
                  
                
A function f:AтЖТB is surjective if every element of B is the image of an element of A тЖТ the range is equal to the codomain
(each element of the codomain has been mapped from at least an element of the domain)
Formal definition:
A function f:AтЖТB is surjective if (тИАyтИИB)(тИГxтИИA)(y=f(x))


Proving that a function is surjective ┬з


                  
                  Warning

A function f:AтЖТB is a bijection if it is injective and surjective

Course description ┬з
This course is a follow-up of the course Procedural Programming. It teaches object-oriented programming in Java. The main topics covered in the course are objects and classes, interfaces and polymorphism, event handling, inheritance, graphic user interfaces, exception handling, and streams.

BSc-Computer-Science/Period-2/BCS1440-Ca.content:
Course description ┬з
The following subjects will be discussed in Calculus: limits and continuity, differential calculus, inverse and transcendental functions, mean value theorem, integral calculus, sequences and series, introduction to differential equations, introduction to multivariable calculus. In addition to the main facts and concepts, problem-solving strategies will be discussed. Both the intuition behind the concepts and their rigorous definitions will be presented along with simple examples of formal mathematical proofs.

Contributing to the notes ┬з
The repository (and notes) are open source, so you can contribute with your notes or suggest changes.
The lecture notes are essentially Markdown files contained in this folder. All lecture notes include mathematical formulas which are taken with LaTex.

You can ignore everything other than the тАЬcontentsтАЭ folder inside of the тАЬnotesтАЭ folder, as it is only code used to translate the markdownfiles into a NextJS website.

To contribute to the notes and/or assignment submissions:

Clone the repository on your local machine

To contribute to the assignment/quizzes

Make a merge request with your changes

To contribute to the lecture notes

Open the content folder in Obsidian
Make a merge request with your edits and notes
